plainMeaning: Praise the stūpa of Supārśva the Jina at Mathurā, the holy spot where — through an ascetic's spiritual power — white banners are said to have risen up overnight.
recitation: taccaṁ niyāṇavāe, seyapaḍāgā nisāi jahiṁ jāyā | khavaga pabhāvā taṁ thuṇi, mahurāi supāsajiṇa thūbhaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. tathyam, 'true, real' (thy → cch, as in satya → sacca).
wordByWord.notes: Read here adverbially ('truly, verily'); some editions could parse it as tṛtīyam ('thirdly'), counting a position in the roll-call. Flagged in notes.

wordByWord.gloss: in the dispute over a niyāṇa (binding resolve / wager-vow)
wordByWord.etymology: ṇiyāṇa < Skt. nidāna ('binding mental resolve, vow with a fixed aim') + vāe < Skt. vāde, locative of vāda ('dispute, contest, debate').
wordByWord.notes: Compound and case are uncertain: vāe may instead be instrumental ('by way of a nidāna'). The line evokes a contest/wager episode at the shrine. See notes.

wordByWord.etymology: seya < Skt. śveta ('white') + paḍāgā < Skt. patākā ('flag, banner'); nom. plural.
wordByWord.notes: White banners are an auspicious sign of victory/sanctity over the shrine.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative of nisā < Skt. niśā ('night'), = niśāyām.

wordByWord.notes: Correlates with the named site in the second half (taṁ … thūbhaṁ).

wordByWord.etymology: Past participle < Skt. jāta ('born, arisen'), from √jan; nom. plural agreeing with paḍāgā.
< Skt. kṣapaka, 'one who wears away / destroys (karma)', from √kṣap; the monk who mounts the kṣapaka-śreṇi.
wordByWord.notes: Here a powerful Jain ascetic whose tapas effected the miracle.

wordByWord.gloss: by the power, through the spiritual influence (of)
wordByWord.etymology: < Skt. prabhāva ('majestic power, influence, efficacy'); ablative/instrumental sense, prabhāvāt.
wordByWord.notes: khavaga-pabhāvā = 'by the ascetic's power' — the agency behind the banners' appearance.
Object of thuṇi; resumes the relative jahiṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: < Skt. √stu, 'to praise' (stuhi); cf. the hymn's refrain thuṇimo.
wordByWord.notes: An imperative/optative of homage — the recurring devotional verb of the garland.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative of Mahurā < Skt. Mathurā (intervocalic th → h).
wordByWord.notes: The ancient Jain centre on the Yamunā (Kankali Tila site).

wordByWord.etymology: supāsa < Skt. Supārśva (7th Tīrthaṅkara) + jiṇa < Skt. jina ('conqueror'); modifies thūbhaṁ.
< Skt. stūpa (Prakrit thūbha/thupa, the form 'thupe' in Mathurā Jain inscriptions); acc. sing.
wordByWord.notes: A domed reliquary-shrine; the Mathurā one was famed as deva-built.

literalTranslation: Truly, in the dispute over a binding vow, where white banners arose by night — by the power of the ascetic — praise that: at Mathurā, the stūpa of Supārśva the Jina.
idiomaticTranslation: Praise that stūpa of Supārśva the Jina at Mathurā — the very place where, so the tale of the binding vow tells, white victory-banners rose overnight by the spiritual power of a Jain ascetic.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the Śatruñjaya cluster (vv.72–75) the garland steps north to the Yamunā and bows to a single, archaeologically real monument: the Supārśva stūpa of Mathurā (mahurāi supāsajiṇa thūbhaṁ). Two features make this verse distinct. First, its object of homage is not a mountain or a living Jina but a thūbha — a domed memorial-shrine, the only stūpa explicitly garlanded in the whole hymn — fixing in verse the tradition that Mathurā preserved a deva-built shrine of the seventh Tīrthaṅkara, later encased in brick. Second, the verse compresses a miracle-legend into a relative clause: jahiṁ … seyapaḍāgā nisāi jāyā, 'where white banners arose by night.' The white banner (seya-paḍāgā) is the stock token of victory and sanctity; its overnight appearance, khavaga pabhāvā — 'by the power of a kṣapaka (an ascetic burning away karma)' — credits the wonder not to ritual machinery but to the tapas of a realised monk. The opening taccaṁ niyāṇavāe ('truly, in the matter of the binding vow/wager') frames the episode as a contest settled by spiritual force. The poet does not narrate; he gestures — naming just enough (vow, night, white banners, ascetic, Mathurā, Supārśva) to summon a story the listener is expected to know, and seals it with the garland's signature imperative thuṇi, 'praise!' Thus a famous site, a doctrinal point about ascetic power, and an act of homage are folded into a single gāthā.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse quietly teaches prabhāva — the efficacy of tapas. The marvel is attributed to a kṣapaka, the technical term for an ascetic ascending the karma-destroying ladder (kṣapaka-śreṇi); the white banners are an outward sign of an inward conquest of karma, not magic for its own sake. It also affirms image- and shrine-veneration (caitya-bhakti): a stūpa, like a Jina image, is a worthy focus of homage because it localises and transmits the Jina's presence. By honouring a memorial rather than a person, the verse extends the hymn's reach to the seventh Tīrthaṅkara, Supārśva, through the relic-shrine that carried his memory across centuries at Mathurā.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When reciting this line, the worshipper bows to a tīrtha most will never visit, and is reminded that genuine spiritual power flows from ascetic discipline, not display. The 'white banner overnight' becomes an inner aspiration: let restraint and tapas, quietly practised, raise their own banner over the mind.

crossReferences.text: Mathurāpurī-kalpa (account of the deva-built Supārśva stūpa later encased in brick under Pārśvanātha)
Mathurā Jain stūpa called deva-nirmita ('god-built') in the Saṁvat 79 (c.156–157 CE) inscription
crossReferences.source: V. A. Smith, The Jain Stūpa and Other Antiquities of Mathurā

sources.title: Jain stupa — deva-nirmita stupa of Suparshvanatha at Mathura; Vividha Tirtha Kalpa Mathurapuri Kalpa
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the verse's subject: the god-built stūpa originally erected for the 7th Tīrthaṅkara Supārśvanātha at Mathurā, later encased in brick, and the inscriptional 'deva-nirmita' claim.

sources.title: Kankali Tila — the excavated Jain stupa mound at Mathura
sources.consultedFor: Archaeological identification and dating of the Mathurā Jain stūpa site honoured in this verse.

sources.title: Vividha Tirtha Kalpa (Jinaprabhasūri)
sources.consultedFor: Provenance of the Mathurā Supārśva-stūpa legend in the medieval Jain pilgrimage-kalpa tradition.

sources.title: Suparshvanatha — the 7th Tīrthaṅkara
sources.consultedFor: Identity of supāsa-jiṇa (Supārśva), the Jina to whom the Mathurā stūpa is dedicated.

notes: The site (mahurāi supāsajiṇa thūbhaṁ = the Supārśva-Jina stūpa at Mathurā) is securely identified and well attested. The micro-legend encoded in the first half is read with lower confidence: (1) 'taccaṁ' is taken as tathyam ('truly'); it could instead be tṛtīyam ('thirdly'). (2) 'niyāṇavāe' is parsed as nidāna + vāde ('in the dispute over a binding vow/wager'), but vāe may be instrumental ('by way of a nidāna'); the exact wager-narrative behind 'white banners arising overnight by the ascetic's power' was not located in a verified critical edition and varies across retellings — treated here as the legend the poet alludes to rather than asserted fact. Orthography of the source (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition) is preserved verbatim. Per-token glosses follow standard Prakrit→Sanskrit phonological correspondences (e.g., śveta→seya, patākā→paḍāgā, stūpa→thūbha, kṣapaka→khavaga).
